local_names.chinese_simplified: 香蕉面包（新加坡咖啡馆厚片）
local_names.chinese_traditional: 香蕉麵包（新加坡咖啡館厚片）
local_names.malay: Roti Pisang — Hirisan Tebal Kafe Singapura

cuisine: Western Café / Home Baking (Singapore indie café context)
region_of_origin: United States (American home-baking tradition, 1930s); widely adopted in Singapore indie cafés from ~2012–2015
food_subgroup: Quick bread (baking soda/powder leavened, no yeast); café baked goods; 1 thick slice ~90g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for banana bread as a distinct food entity. Closest parent would be a quick bread or sweet bread category. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Banana quick bread (café slice) — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy for quick breads
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBZ used as closest parent for banana-based quick bread; no exact FoodEx2 code for Singapore café banana bread slice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for banana bread as a food product. Null retained.

serving.description: 1 thick slice (~90g) — standard Singapore café serving from a standard loaf
serving.natural_language_portion: One thick slice of banana bread as served at Singapore indie cafés, approximately 90g, yielding approximately 300 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — component calculation from FDC 172686 (all-purpose flour), FDC 173944 (raw banana), FDC 173410 (butter, salted), FDC 169640 (white granulated sugar)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy component-level calculation using standard banana bread recipe proportions (flour, ripe banana, butter, sugar, eggs). No single USDA entry for banana bread as a composite dish; values represent a weighted recipe calculation. Confidence is medium. Singapore HPB database does not contain a banana bread entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this Western-origin baked good.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of banana bread estimated from analogous refined wheat flour quick bread; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singapore café banana bread exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~70 estimated from the closest analogous food: refined wheat flour-based quick bread / banana bread entries in the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Banana bread is primarily a refined wheat flour product; the banana contributes natural sugars but does not substantially lower the GI relative to the flour base. GL per 90g serving = (70 × 47.0g available carbohydrate per 90g) / 100 = ~32.9, rounded to 31.5 per input source calculation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~200mg per 90g slice) and saturated fat (~5g per slice) from butter
hypertension.guidance: Sodium per slice (~200mg) is moderate and within single-item limits for hypertensive patients; however, the saturated fat from butter (~5g per slice) and cholesterol (~50mg per slice) warrant caution for patients with concurrent cardiovascular risk — limit to occasional consumption and avoid adding extra butter as a spread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high glycemic load (~31.5 per 90g slice) driven by refined wheat flour and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Banana bread carries a high GI (~70) and a high GL (~31.5 per slice), making it unsuitable as a regular food choice for T2DM patients; if consumed at social occasions, limit to half a slice (~45g, GL ~15), and prefer almond-flour, no-added-sugar variants which can achieve GI ~55 and GL ~12 per slice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.6g per 100g / ~5g per slice) and trans fat (~0.1g per 100g) from butter, plus cholesterol (~55mg per 100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~5g per 90g slice, ~25% of a 20g daily saturated fat limit) and the presence of trans fat (~0.09g per slice) are relevant for cardiovascular disease patients; banana bread should be consumed only occasionally and portion-controlled, with preference for oil-based (canola or olive oil) recipes that reduce saturated fat by approximately 50%.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory food profile: high GI refined wheat flour, added sugar, and moderate saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The combination of refined wheat flour, added sugar (high GI/GL), and moderate saturated fat creates a pro-inflammatory dietary profile; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption and consider anti-inflammatory substitutions such as almond flour and reduced sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana bread is a quick bread (leavened with baking soda or baking powder, not yeast) made from very ripe mashed bananas, refined wheat flour, sugar, butter, and eggs. It is baked in a loaf tin and served in thick slices (~90g) at Singapore indie cafés such as The Lokal, Habitat Coffee, and Department of Caffeine. The texture is dense, moist, and tender with a golden-brown crust; the flavour is sweet and banana-forward with a buttery richness. Despite its name, banana bread is nutritionally closer to cake than bread: per 90g slice it provides approximately 300 kcal, 4g protein, 10g fat (~5g saturated), 47g carbohydrate, and 24g sugar (roughly half from the banana and half from added sugar). The glycemic index is high (~70) and the glycemic load per slice is high (~31.5), driven primarily by refined wheat flour and added sugar — the banana ingredient does not meaningfully lower the GI. Sodium is moderate (~200mg per slice). Banana bread has a well-documented 'health halo' effect from the banana ingredient that is not nutritionally justified for glycaemic management. It is unsuitable as a regular food for T2DM patients and should be consumed only occasionally by those managing cardiovascular risk due to its saturated fat content. Almond-flour, no-added-sugar variants available at some Singapore health cafés offer a meaningfully better nutritional profile (GI ~55, GL ~12 per slice). Banana bread became popular in the United States during the Great Depression (~1930s) as a way to use overripe bananas; it entered Singapore's independent café scene from approximately 2012–2015.
common_user_queries: is banana bread good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy component-level calculation (weighted recipe approach) rather than a direct laboratory analysis or a single USDA composite entry for banana bread. GI is an analogous estimate from the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008) rather than a direct measurement of this specific product. Singapore HPB database does not contain a banana bread entry. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this banana bread recipe calculation is not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; individual component entries (flour, banana, butter) do not provide a reliable aggregated MUFA/PUFA figure for the baked composite. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the baked composite is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component calculation used. All other micronutrient values are carried from the USDA SR Legacy component calculation with medium confidence. GI ~70 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement of Singapore café banana bread has not been published. GL per serving calculated as (70 × 47.0g available carbohydrate per 90g slice) / 100 ≈ 32.9; the input source reports 31.5 which is retained as the primary value. Banana bread is a Western-origin food adopted into Singapore café culture; multilingual names provided reflect Singapore's four official languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil) plus Hindi as required by Project Veritas multilingual rules for Singapore-context dishes.
